setup.context: Let me tell you about a tiger who thought he was a goat...

paragraphs.text: A pregnant tigress died while hunting. Her cub, just born, was found by a herd of goats. They adopted him. Fed him grass. Taught him to bleat. The cub grew up believing he was a goat.
Years passed. The tiger ate grass like a goat, walked like a goat, made goat sounds. He was thin, weak, utterly unconvincing as a goat—but he tried his best. The other goats accepted him.
One day, a wild tiger attacked the herd. The goats fled in terror. But this goat-tiger didn't run fast enough. The wild tiger caught him, confused. 'Why didn't you fight? What kind of tiger are you?'
The goat-tiger bleated, 'I'm not a tiger! I'm a goat!' The wild tiger dragged him to a pond and forced him to look at his reflection. 'LOOK at yourself. You are not a goat. You never were.'
The goat-tiger looked. For the first time, he saw his own face—powerful, beautiful, unmistakably tiger. Something stirred. He opened his mouth and for the first time in his life... he roared.

reflection.insight: You are the tiger who thinks you're a goat. You've been conditioned to believe you're limited, ordinary, bound by circumstances. But look in the mirror. You are consciousness itself wearing a human costume. You have always been the tiger.
reflection.question: What false identity have you accepted? What would happen if you looked at your reflection clearly?

applications.connection: You're not small because you ARE small. You're small because you've learned to be small. It's not your nature—it's your conditioning.
People fear their own greatness more than they fear failure. What if you allowed yourself to roar?
Enlightenment is not becoming something new. It's recognizing what you always were—the tiger, not the goat.
